sections.paragraph:
only in plur. כְּלָיֹות const. כִּלְיֹות f.

(1) the kidneys, reins. Exo 29:13, 22 Exo 29:22 Job 16:13. חֶלֶב כִּלְיֹות אֵילִים “the fat of the kidneys of rams,” Isa 34:6 comp. Deu 32:14.

(2) meton, used of the inmost mind, as the seat of the desires and affections. Jer 11:20, בֹּחֵן כְּלָיֹות וָלֵב “(God) tries the reins and the heart.” Jerem. 17:10 20:12 Psa 7:10 Job 19:27, בָּלוּ בִלְיֹותַי “my reins (i.e. my inmost soul) have wasted away.” Psa 73:21 Pro 23:16. Chald. sing. כֻּלְיָא, Arab. كُلْيَةُ, rarely and inaccurately كِلْيَةُ id. Schultens supposed the reins to be so called, because of their being double; compare כִּלְאַיִם, كِلاَُ (which is unsuitable because כלאים signifies rather things diverse in kind, and in Arabic this word is used in sing. dual and plural); Aben Ezra and Bochart considered them to be so called from the idea of desire, longing, comp. Job 19:27, but I do not know why כִּלְיָה should not be simply the fem. of the noun בְּלִי and thus signify properly instrument, vessel (Gefäß), just as physicians call the veins and arteries, vessels.
